title: Structure of a psychodiagnostic test battery for children
abstract:
STRUCTUR.E OF A PSYCHODIAGNOSTIC TEST BATTERY 
FOR CHILDREN 
ROBERT ZAGAR AND JACK ARBIT 
North- 
Uniustsity 
IUinois I&&& 
of Technology 
Compared principal component analysis with hierarchical cluster analysie 
of a sychodiagnostic test batte 
for children, which included the Wechsler 
IntelEgence Scale for Children%vised, the Raven Progressive Matrices, 
the Wide Range Achievement Test, the Wepman Audito 
Discrimination 
Test, the Gates-MacGinitie Reading Test, the Gray Oral Zading Teat and 
the Huelsman Word Discrimination Teat. Two hundred and thirteen chil- 
dren (149 males and 64 females) with a mean age of 11.17 were hted. The 
results are discussed within a multidimensional framework of cognitive 
abilities. The structure of the battery of tests included general intelligence, 
auditory and visual learning, academic achievement, and auditory dig 
crimination. 
JOTHAY FRIEDLAND' 
l'sychodiagnostic test batteries have come into wide use in order to survey 
intellectual functions both qualitatively and quantitatively. The basic psycho- 
logical test battery for children serves to evaluate the ability of the child to process 
information. Attempts to define the nature and organization of these mental 
abilities primarily have employed factor-analytic procedures. Spearman (1927) 
demonstrated an hierarchical organization of mental abilities which included a 
general factor of intelligence and group factors common to broad classes of abjlities. 
Among the test batteries for use with children are the Wechsler Intelligence 
Scale for Children-Revised (WISC-R) (Wechsler, 1974), the Wide Range Achieve- 
ment Test (WRAT) (Jastak & Jastak, 1965) the Stanford-Binet Intelligence Test 
(Terman & Merrill, 1960), the Halstead-Reitan Neuropsychological Test Battery 
for Children (Hslstead, 1947; Reitan & Davison, 1974), and the Illinois Test of 
Psycholinguistic Ability (Kirk & Kirk, 1971). Defining the intellectual functioning 
and organization of mental abilities as measured in these test batteries in terms of 
factors or clusters that emerge serves both a descriptive and predictive purpose. 
Reitan and Davison (1974) grouped mental abilities into homogeneous and 
common clusters : Auditory perception, visual-spatial and sequential ability, verbal 
ability, incidental memory, academic ability, tactual-perceptual performance and 
tactual-motor problem solving. Kirk and Kirk (1971) presented a sensory input and 
expressive output model of cognitive ability. The major modes of reception are 
auditory and visual, output is vocal and motor. Lezak (1976) discussed classes of 
intellectual functions in terms of reception, memory, thinking, expression, and 
mental activity. 
The present study assessed the principal component structure of a psycho- 
diagnostic test battery for children in order to assist in understanding the descrip- 
tiva components in the diagnosis of intellectual functioning of children. The met.hods 
chosen were both non-linear hierarchical cluster analysis and confirmatory principal 
component analysis. Five factors or clusters were hypothesized : (1) general in- 
telligence; (2) academic achievement; (3) auditory discrimination ; (4) visual die- 
crimination; and (5) verbal ability. 
METHOD 
Subjects and Procedure 
Two hundred and thirteen children from the Institute for Psychological 
Services of the Illinois Institute of Technology were administered a battery of 
IThe authors wish to e 
reBB their gratitude to the staff of the Institute for Ps chological Ser- 
vices, Illinois Institute of ghnology, Chicago, Illinois, and in particular Ruth &obbins, Ph.D. 
313 
 314 
Journal of Clinical Psychology, January, 1980, Vol. $6, No. 1. 
tests that included the WISC-R, the Ravens Progressive Matrices, the Wepman 
Auditory Discrimination Test, the WRAT, the Gray Oral Reading Test, the 
Huelsman Visual Discrimination Test, and the Gates-MacGinitie Reading Test. 
There were 149 males and 64 females with an average age of 11.17 (SD = 3.03). 
The subtest scores of the WISC-R and the test scores of the other tests were 
submitted to confirmatory principal component analysia and then rotated Varimax 
using Kaiser normalization. Eigenvalues were set to 1.0, and unities were inserted 
into the diagonals. In the second step of the analysis, the scores were submitted to 
hierarchical cluster analysis to identify independent clusters using Cronbach’s 
coefficient alpha (the mean split-half reliabilities) and an estimate of the coefficient 
beta (the worst split-half reliability) to determine the overall goodness of fit. This 
approach identifies composite clusters that are both internally consistent and 
relatively independent (Gray 6 Revelle, 1972). 
RESULTS 
Five factors emerged from the principal component analysis and accounted 
for 73.7% of the variance. They were named general intelligence, academic 
achievement, auditory and visual learning, auditory discrimination, and motoric 
problem solving.

Services of the Illinois Institute of Technology were administered a battery of

tests that included the WISC-R, the Ravens Progressive Matrices, the Wepman
Auditory Discrimination Test, the WRAT, the Gray Oral Reading Test, the
Huelsman Visual Discrimination Test, and the Gates-MacGinitie Reading Test.
There were 149 males and 64 females with an average age of 11.17 (SD = 3.03).
The subtest scores of the WISC-R and the test scores of the other tests were
submitted to confirmatory principal component analysia and then rotated Varimax
using Kaiser normalization. Eigenvalues were set to 1.0, and unities were inserted
into the diagonals. In the second step of the analysis, the scores were submitted to

Five factors emerged from the principal component analysis and accounted
for 73.7% of the variance. They were named general intelligence, academic
achievement, auditory and visual learning, auditory discrimination, and motoric
Hierarchical cluster analysis may be thought of as a simple factoring pro-
cedure in which unit weighted composites are organized within a cluster. The
hierarchical cluster analysis of the 22 variables (Table 2) demonstrated the exis-
tence of five clusters: (1) general intelligence; (2) auditory and visual learning;
(3) short-term memory; (4) academic achievement ; and (5) auditory discrimination.
The summary table (Table 3) provides the alphas (Cronbach’s internal consistency),
betw (worst split-half reliability, gammas (upper bound of test reliability), average
correlation within clusters, sums of squared correlations within the cluster, relative
sums of squared correlations within the cluster, and goodness of fit tests. These
measures indicate the intercluster similarity through the correlation of covariance
between clusters. Clusters are combined to define composites by the sum of the
unit weighted items within each subcluster.
Journal of Clinical Psychology, January, 1980, Vol. 36, No. 1.

OrthogonagI goodnees of fit = .573.
DEVIATIONS OF PSYCHODIAQNOSTIC
The means and standard deviation for all tests along with the correlation
matrix are presented in Tables 4 and 5.
Journal of Clinical Psychology, January, 1980, Vol. 36, No. 1.
On the basis of the Varimax loadings of the principal component analysis, this
battery of psychodiagnostic tests for children appears to assess five classes of
cognitive functions : General intelligence, academic achievement, auditory and
visual learning, auditory discrimination, and motoric problem solving. This division
of mental ability factors fits well into the nature of intelligence as an hierarchical
The hierarchical cluster analysis essentially confirmed the presence of this
structure with the motoric problem solving factor and the addition of a short-term
The purpose of a battery of psychodiagnostic tests for children is the accurate
descriDtion of cognitive and Derformance functioning over a wide sample of tasks.
The analysis of iuch a test -battery indicates the factors of general -intelligence,
academic achievement, auditory and visual learning, and auditory discrimination
as replicated by the two techniques that were employed. The results suggest that
behavioral measurements oriented toward evaluation of a broad range of abilities
provide objective evidence for the hierarchical nature of intelligence and ultimately
the possibility of drawing conclusions about brain-behavior relationships within
the context of the functional organization of abilities in children. The battery of
diagnostic tests included within this analysis samples a wide variety of abilities
in a relatively short period of testing time with relatively little testing equipment
and results in a clear, distinct evaluation of a wide variety of mental abilities.
